title: Limits of Prompting
concept: Prompting is flexible but consumes context window tokens, is highly sensitive to wording variations, and typically underperforms dedicated fine-tuning on narrow, high-volume classification tasks. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: Evaluation & Practice.'

exercises.prompt: Which of the following is a key limitation of relying solely on prompting?

options.text: It consumes short-term memory (context window tokens) on every call.
It permanently alters the model's weights.
It requires weeks of heavy GPU training before you can try it.

exercises.explain: Prompting is great for quick prototyping, but it uses up limited context window space and becomes expensive at scale.

cards.title: The Limits of Magic
cards.body: Prompting feels like magic because you can change how an AI behaves just by rewriting a sentence. But what happens when your prompt gets too long, costs too much, or breaks when you change a single word? Let's explore the hidden limits of prompting.

cards.title: What are Prompting Limits?
cards.body: Prompting has limits. Every instruction you give uses up 'context window tokens' (the model's short-term memory), costs money per run, and can easily break if the wording changes slightly. For narrow, high-volume tasks, prompting often loses to dedicated fine-tuning.

cards.title: The Temp Worker vs. The Specialist
cards.body: Imagine hiring a temp worker who has to read a massive rulebook from scratch every single morning. That is prompting. Fine-tuning, on the other hand, is like hiring an experienced specialist who already has the entire rulebook permanently wired into their brain.

cards.title: The Cost of Repetition
cards.body: If you prompt an LLM with 50 examples of how to tag customer support emails, you pay for those 50 examples *every single time* a customer sends a new email. If you receive millions of emails daily, this becomes incredibly expensive and slow compared to a fine-tuned model.

cards.title: Myth: The Perfect Prompt is Robust
cards.body: Many think that writing the 'perfect' prompt solves every AI problem forever. In reality, prompts are highly sensitive. Even a small change in punctuation or ordering can cause the model to fail, making them fragile for critical production systems.

cards.title: Memory Comparison
cards.body: Comparing how prompting and fine-tuning handle the memory space (context window) during tasks.

left.text: Requires instructions and examples inside the context window for every request.

right.text: Learns the pattern directly in the model's weights, keeping the context window free.

exercises.prompt: What is a major financial downside of relying on long prompts with many examples for high-volume tasks?

options.text: You have to pay a one-time fee to unlock the prompt.
You pay for all the instruction tokens on every single request.
Prompting requires purchasing expensive local graphics hardware.
The model becomes completely free after the first thousand tokens.

exercises.explain: Because prompting relies on short-term memory, you must send all instructions and examples with every single API call, leading to high recurring token costs.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Prompts are highly robust and will always yield the exact same output format even if you slightly change the wording.
exercises.explain: Even minor changes in formatting or word order can drastically change how a prompted model responds.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing term about prompting limitations.
exercises.sentence: Putting too many instructions in a prompt consumes valuable context window tokens.
exercises.explain: Every word or character you include in a prompt counts as a token, which eats up the model's limited short-term memory (context window).

exercises.prompt: Identify which system is relying on prompting versus fine-tuning.

options.text: System A is fine-tuned because it is very short.
System A is using prompting (in-context examples), while System B is fine-tuned.
Both systems are using identical prompting techniques.

exercises.explain: System B represents fine-tuning, where the model is pre-trained on the task, requiring only the input text. System A is using in-context prompting, which requires sending instructions every time.

exercises.prompt: Why would a company choose to fine-tune a model instead of using a long, detailed prompt for a high-volume sentiment classification system?

options.text: It is easier to set up than simple prompting.
It permanently updates model weights, saving token space and improving consistency.
It requires zero training data or examples.
It makes the model run faster for general-purpose conversation.

exercises.explain: Fine-tuning is better for high-volume, narrow tasks because it doesn't waste precious context tokens repeating the same instructions over and over again! Next up: Evaluation & Practice.
